Dear tIr. Robeck:
This is in response to your request for a confirnation of
telephone advice regarding the campaign provisions of the
poliLicaL Reform Act (the "Act"1.17 Since your request does not
name a specific elected official 
on whose behalf you have
requested this advice, $/e can,only provide the follor.ring informal
reJponse= to your questions . 2 /
This letter confirms that your letter dated June 25, 1990 ac-
curately summarizes in part the telephone advice I provided to you
on that day concerning the definition of rrcommitteerr under the
Act. However, soroe nodification of the advice j.s necessary to
ensure that such a procedure is consistent Irith the requirements
of the Act.
First, in your question and in item one of your letter you
refer to the sponsor of the fundraiser as a rrgroup. rt Please note
that an entity (including a "commj,tteerr) may act as an
intermediary for receiving contributions for a candidate or
committee. In this case, the interrnediary must transfer or

ences are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated.

mission regulations appear at 2 Californj-a Code of Regulations

Tit1e 2, Division 6 of the caLifornia code of Regulations.
2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the

83114; Regulation L8329 (c) (3). )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322'5660

transmit all of the contributions to the candidate or cornmittee on
whose behalf they have been solicited. 
The intermediary is
required to provide all required infornation to the candidate or
cornmittee concerning the true sources of the contributions.
However, if a rrgroup,rr or any other entity, receives
contributions, and it uses any portion of those contributions to
make expenditures on behatf of a candidate or committee, the group
or other entity will neet the definition of a t'recipient
comnittee" once it receives $1,ooo in contributions. 
(BelI Advice
Letter, No. A-88-166, copy enclosed.)
In iten three of your letter you state that the sponsor will
be keeping records rras though there was a reporting requirement' "
However, where the sponsor is the internediary, the sponsor is
reouired to disclose the true source of any contributions.
Fuither, the recipient must disclose both the intermediary and the
true contributor. 
(Sections 84302 and a43o2.5i Regulation
fa432.5, copy enclosed.) Thus, there i,s a reporting requirement
associ.ated with the fundraiser.
Finally, in item four you refer to the making of in-kind
contributions to the candidates. As discussed above, if the group
uses any portion of the noney received to make expenditures on
behal,f of a candidate or committee, the group will no longer be
considered an rrintermediary, " but will meet the definition of a
ttrecipient comrnitteert when it has received $1,000 for this
purpose. In addition, if roenbers of the group spend S1,000 or
more of their own money or resources to make expenditures on
behalf of a candidate or committee, a rrrecipient conmitteett will
be created. (Bell Advice Letter, supra.)
Finally, pLease be aware that based on the circunstances of
the situation you have described, if the group qualifies as a
"recipient committee, " it appears that it rnay be 'rcontrol-ledrr by
the candidate on whose behalf the fundraiser is being he1d. A
candidate may not control more than one committee for each
specific office sought or hel-d. Therefore, steps must be taken to
ensure that the group does not qualify as a recipient committee.
If you have any further questj.ons reqarding this matter,
please feel free to contact this off i.ce at (916) 322-59oL.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral Counsel
John w. Wal,lace
Counsel-, Legal Division

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor sought confirmation of telephone advice regarding the definition of 'committee' under the Political Reform Act, specifically concerning fundraiser procedures, intermediary responsibilities, reporting requirements, and in-kind contributions for a candidate.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An entity acting as an intermediary for contributions must transfer all funds to the intended candidate/committee and disclose true sources; if the group receives $1,000+ or spends $1,000+ of members' own money on candidate expenditures, it becomes a 'recipient committee' subject to reporting requirements and cannot be controlled by the candidate if that candidate already controls another committee for the same office.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document explicitly labeled as informal assistance response; no verbatim QUESTION section but clear summary of requestor's questions in body; CONCLUSION synthesized from detailed guidance throughout letter; footnotes indicate informal advice lacks immunity of formal opinions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor sought confirmation of telephone advice regarding the definition of 'committee' under the Political Reform Act, specifically concerning fundraiser procedures, intermediary responsibilities, reporting requirements, and in-kind contributions for a candidate.

CONCLUSION: An entity acting as an intermediary for contributions must transfer all funds to the intended candidate/committee and disclose true sources; if the group receives $1,000+ or spends $1,000+ of members' own money on candidate expenditures, it becomes a 'recipient committee' subject to reporting requirements and cannot be controlled by the candidate if that candidate already controls another committee for the same office.

embedding.summary: FPPC informal advice letter confirming and modifying telephone advice regarding committee definitions, intermediary status, reporting requirements for fundraisers, and restrictions on candidate control of multiple committees under the Political Reform Act.
